params.text:
Evacuating a care home

Evacuating a care home is very different from evacuating an office, because many residents cannot simply walk out. Care homes are built and designed for progressive horizontal evacuation. Instead of immediately taking everyone outside, staff move residents away from the fire, through fire-resisting doors, into an adjoining protected compartment on the same floor. There they are in relative safety and can wait while the fire is dealt with, or be moved further to complete safety if needed. The aim is to get people beyond a fire-resisting barrier quickly – usually within a couple of minutes.

This approach means residents are not exposed to the dangers of moving large numbers of frail or immobile people down stairs unless it is truly necessary. Some homes also use a "stay put" or delayed strategy for parts of the building, where residents remain in a fire-protected area rather than being fully evacuated – but this is only ever used where the building design and fire risk assessment specifically allow it. Always follow your home's own agreed plan.

PEEPs: Personal Emergency Evacuation Plans

Every person who could not evacuate independently should have a Personal Emergency Evacuation Plan (PEEP). A PEEP is a written, individual plan that records exactly how that person will be helped to safety – for example how many staff are needed, what mobility or evacuation equipment (such as an evacuation sledge or ski-sheet) is required, any sensory or communication needs, and where they should be moved to.

Because people's needs change, PEEPs must be reviewed and kept up to date. Make sure you know where the PEEPs are kept and understand the plans for the people you support before an emergency happens – not during one.

params.question: What is the main principle of "progressive horizontal evacuation" in a care home?

answers.text: Moving residents away from the fire into a protected area on the same floor, rather than immediately outside

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Residents are moved horizontally through fire-resisting doors into an adjoining protected compartment on the same level, where they are safe while the fire is tackled.

answers.text: Taking every resident down the stairs and out of the building at once

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Moving many frail residents down stairs immediately is dangerous and is avoided where possible. They are first moved horizontally to a protected area on the same floor.

answers.text: Leaving residents in their rooms and locking the doors

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. The aim is to move people away from the fire, through a fire-resisting barrier, to a place of relative safety on the same floor.

answers.text: Evacuating only staff first and returning for residents later

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Staff support residents to safety – no one is left behind. Residents are moved horizontally to a protected area.
